phase.title: User & Group Enumeration
phase.summary: Identify the current security context, group memberships, other accounts, and the password policy that frames every later attack.

nodes.title: whoami /all
nodes.summary: Dump SID, group memberships, and token privileges in one shot.
nodes.description_md: The output of `whoami /all` is the single most valuable file in a Windows engagement: SID, all group SIDs (including S-1-5-32-544 Administrators if you happen to be admin), and the enabled/disabled token privileges. The `Privileges` section directly feeds Phase 3.
nodes.detection_hints: Repeated `whoami /all` from a non-interactive session is a strong PrivEsc pre-cursor signal.

nodes.title: Local groups & members
nodes.summary: Enumerate the local groups and who is in them — especially Administrators, Backup Operators, Power Users.
nodes.description_md: Membership in Backup Operators (SeBackupPrivilege) or DnsAdmins is a direct privesc path.

nodes.title: Other local accounts
nodes.summary: Find accounts beyond your own — service accounts, leftover test users, and AD accounts cached locally.
nodes.description_md: Disabled accounts can sometimes be re-enabled, and old service accounts often have weak passwords.

snippets.title: Get-LocalUser
snippets.code: Get-LocalUser | Select-Object Name, Enabled, LastLogon, PasswordRequired

nodes.title: Active sessions
nodes.summary: Other interactive sessions = potential token theft targets (Phase 11).
nodes.description_md: If an admin is logged on, `SeImpersonatePrivilege` + named-pipe coercion can give you their token.

nodes.title: Password & lockout policy
nodes.summary: Set the bounds for any password-guessing you may want to do laterally.
nodes.description_md: If lockout threshold is 0, online brute force is on the table. Otherwise plan offline cracking.

nodes.title: Local administrator check
nodes.summary: Confirm whether your account is a local admin (even if at Medium integrity).
nodes.description_md: Membership in the local Administrators group means a UAC bypass is enough for full control. Check group membership and the well-known SID `S-1-5-32-544`.

nodes.title: SID enumeration
nodes.summary: Map account names to SIDs for ACL and token analysis.
nodes.description_md: SIDs are needed to interpret object ACLs and token groups. Enumerate local accounts and their SIDs; a RID of 500 marks the built-in Administrator.

nodes.title: Token group membership
nodes.summary: Inspect every group SID in your token, including restricted/deny-only.
nodes.description_md: `whoami /groups` shows the full token — including groups granted via nested membership and the integrity label. A deny-only Administrators entry signals a filtered (UAC) token.

nodes.title: Cached Kerberos tickets
nodes.summary: List cached tickets that may be exportable for Pass-the-Ticket.
nodes.description_md: `klist` shows tickets in the current logon session. On a multi-user/host with elevated context, cached TGTs/TGSs can be extracted (Rubeus/Mimikatz) and reused — a bridge into AD.

references.title: MITRE T1558 — Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets

nodes.title: Account & lockout policy
nodes.summary: Read password/lockout policy before any on-box password attempts.
nodes.description_md: `net accounts` reveals minimum password length, lockout threshold, and lockout duration. Essential before spraying local accounts so you don't lock anyone out.

nodes.title: Recently created / privileged users
nodes.summary: Spot interesting accounts and their last-logon timestamps.
nodes.description_md: Service accounts and recently-created users are high-value. `net user <name>` shows group membership, last logon, and sometimes a password hint in the comment/description field.

nodes.title: Interactive sessions
nodes.summary: Enumerate logged-on sessions for token theft opportunities.
nodes.description_md: Other users' sessions mean their tokens live in memory. With SeDebug/admin you can impersonate them. `query session` / `qwinsta` lists active and disconnected sessions.

nodes.title: Passwords in account fields
nodes.summary: Check description/comment fields where admins sometimes stash passwords.
nodes.description_md: Lazy admins put passwords in the user **description** or comment. Enumerate every local account's description; in AD the same is true of the `description` attribute.

nodes.title: Clipboard & recent activity
nodes.summary: Peek at clipboard contents and recently opened files for secrets.
nodes.description_md: The clipboard frequently holds freshly pasted passwords. Recent-files and RDP history reveal where credentials and sensitive material live.
